title: CachingTest LoadMultiWithConfig_race import_model call count mismatch on Windows
signature: Actual function call count doesnt match EXPECT_CALL import_model Expected called times Actual called times unsatisfied and active|Flaky Test

failed_jobs.name: C++ unit tests / C++ unit tests

key_errors:
caching_test.cpp(2667): error: Actual function call count doesn't match EXPECT_CALL(*mockPlugin, import_model(A<std::istream&>(), _))...
         Expected: to be called 7 times
           Actual: called 6 times - unsatisfied and active

caching_test.cpp(312): error: Value of: Mock::VerifyAndClearExpectations(mockPlugin.get())
  Actual: false
Expected: true
root_cause: The LoadMultiWithConfig_race test uses a race-based retry loop and a mock expectation for import_model calls. The race condition under Windows timing means the mock sometimes receives fewer calls than expected (6 instead of 7) within the allotted window. This is a known intermittent failure (3rd occurrence) where the MULTI plugin caching race test expectation count does not account for scheduling jitter on Windows CI runners.
reproduction_steps: Run `ov_unit_tests --gtest_filter=CachingTest/CachingTest.LoadMultiWithConfig_race/ByModelName_testCache` on a Windows runner; the failure is intermittent and timing-dependent.
recommended_actions: Consider widening the expected call count range in the mock (e.g., EXPECT_CALL with AtLeast instead of exact count)
Re-run the failed CI job — as a flaky test, a simple re-run is likely to pass
Track and potentially quarantine this test if it continues to fail intermittently
prevention_strategies: Replace exact EXPECT_CALL count matchers with range matchers (AtLeast/AtMost) for race tests, or add retry logic within the test itself to accommodate OS scheduling variance on Windows.
historical_context: This exact pattern (signature_hash 43e136cf67333978) has been seen twice before: run 29234771303 on 2026-07-13 (PR #36488) and run 29341562536 on 2026-07-14 (PR #36872). Both prior occurrences were resolved by re-running the failing job.
